case_name: Acetochlor removal from river-influenced surface water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for acetochlor-contaminated surface water intended for drinking water supply under moderate cost and conventional operating constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal water utility draws from a surface water source affected seasonally by agricultural runoff from corn-growing areas. Monitoring has identified acetochlor as the primary organic micropollutant of concern, with natural organic matter and turbidity varying during storm events. The utility already operates conventional clarification and filtration and prefers well-established upgrades rather than highly specialized or energy-intensive systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should reliably reduce acetochlor to a very low level suitable for drinking water production while meeting normal drinking water treatment expectations for particulate and microbial control.
secondary_goals: use proven treatment technology with conventional plant integration
maintain moderate operating complexity and chemical handling
limit energy use and avoid unnecessarily complex membrane systems
provide resilience to seasonal organic matter and turbidity fluctuations

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: conventional sludge handling is available and periodic spent carbon replacement or reactivation is acceptable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable operation is required during runoff-driven water quality changes

core_unit_functions.coagulation: destabilize particles and remove part of natural organic matter that competes for downstream adsorption capacity
core_unit_functions.flocculation: form settleable flocs for solids and associated organic matter removal
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: separate bulk suspended solids and reduce particulate loading
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: polish clarified water and protect downstream adsorption media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb acetochlor and other hydrophobic organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: acetochlor is the primary treatment target and activated carbon is a well-established control method for hydrophobic pesticides in drinking water treatment
surface water matrix contains moderate DOC and turbidity, so upstream clarification improves carbon bed performance and extends media life
the utility already has conventional treatment capability, making post-filtration GAC a practical and proven upgrade
this train balances micropollutant removal, microbial protection, and manageable operational complexity
potential_risks: high seasonal DOC may shorten GAC run time and require more frequent media replacement or reactivation
breakthrough can occur if carbon monitoring is not maintained
clarification upsets during storm events can increase downstream adsorber fouling risk

core_unit_functions.coagulation: reduce turbidity and part of organic matter before final treatment
core_unit_functions.flocculation: aggregate suspended particles into removable flocs
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: provide particle polishing and stable finished water quality
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: adsorb acetochlor through dose-based control, especially during episodic contamination periods
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide pathogen inactivation and distribution system protection

applicability_rationale: PAC is a conventional and flexible option for intermittent pesticide events in surface water
dose can be adjusted seasonally when runoff-driven acetochlor concentrations rise
the process fits plants familiar with chemical feed systems and conventional solids handling
it can be attractive where contamination is seasonal rather than year-round
potential_risks: performance depends strongly on contact time, mixing, and timely dose adjustment
natural organic matter competes with acetochlor for adsorption sites and can increase PAC demand
additional solids loading may increase sludge production and handling needs

core_unit_functions.coagulation: remove particulate matter and reduce part of the background organic load
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize some trace organics and improve biodegradability of dissolved organic matter
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: combine adsorption and biologically assisted removal/polishing of biodegradable organics
core_unit_functions.filtration: retain carbon fines and remaining suspended matter

applicability_rationale: ozone plus BAC is a recognized advanced drinking water treatment approach for taste, odor, and some micropollutant control
the train can provide broader organic matter management benefits than adsorption alone
it may be suitable when the utility wants added resilience to seasonal source water deterioration
however it is more complex than needed for a straightforward acetochlor-focused case
potential_risks: acetochlor oxidation may be incomplete without sufficient ozone exposure and process optimization
bromate formation risk must be evaluated if bromide is present even at low levels
operator skill and monitoring requirements are higher than for simple GAC addition

core_unit_functions.coagulation: lower solids and colloidal loading before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: reject acetochlor and a broad range of dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance in permeate

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes can technically control acetochlor and provide broad-spectrum contaminant reduction
they may be considered when multiple dissolved contaminants also drive treatment decisions
for this case, the source water issue is primarily one pesticide with manageable matrix effects, so membranes are not the most economical conventional choice
potential_risks: high capital and operating cost compared with activated carbon options
concentrate management creates a significant residuals burden
membrane fouling risk increases with variable surface water quality

core_unit_functions.conventional_clarification: remove turbidity and part of particulate and colloidal organic matter

applicability_rationale: this reflects the baseline treatment that many surface water plants already use
it can improve general water quality but does not directly target dissolved acetochlor effectively
it is included as a low-ranked benchmark to show that conventional solids removal alone is not the preferred response for pesticide control
potential_risks: insufficient removal of dissolved acetochlor is likely
finished water compliance margin for the target contaminant may be unreliable during runoff events
source water DOC and pesticide pulses can pass through the train with limited attenuation

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based_processes should rank at the top for acetochlor in surface water
conventional clarification alone should rank low because it is not a strong dissolved pesticide barrier
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first under moderate cost and conventional operation constraints

must_reflect: preference for proven municipal drinking water treatment technologies
moderate operational capability with conventional clarification and filtration already available
avoidance of unnecessarily high-energy or high-complexity solutions when adsorption can address the main contaminant

must_contain: the role of activated carbon for hydrophobic pesticide removal
the effect of natural organic matter and turbidity on adsorption performance and pretreatment needs
why conventional solids removal alone is insufficient for dissolved acetochlor
why membrane options rank lower despite technical capability

should_mention: competition from DOC or NOM for adsorption sites
breakthrough monitoring and carbon replacement needs
storm-event variability and seasonal contaminant spikes
residuals or sludge impacts for PAC-containing systems
